preamble: The transformation of the voiceless bilabial plosive /p/ into the velar nasal /ŋ/ is a notable phonetic shift that involves substantial changes in the articulation of the sound. This type of change, while not universally common across all language families, is attested in specific historical linguistic contexts.
phoneticEffects: This shift involves a change in the place of articulation from bilabial to velar (backing), a change in the manner of articulation from plosive to nasal (nasalization), and a change in voicing from voiceless to voiced, as nasals are typically voiced

examples.note: An example of /p/ shifting to /ŋ/ in Sumerian, as part of a documented phonetic rule.

sources: Kenanidis, G., & Papakitsos, E. C. (2018). Going to the Root: Paving the Way to Reconstruct the Language of Homo-Sapiens. International Linguistics Research, 1(2), 1-19.
